sentences.text: My name now is Andrew Gardner.
I was born as [?
Andrei ?] Gruenfeld in Hungary in 1925.
I was the fourth of five children, five boys, and grew up as a young child in a very happy family, very orthodox.
My father had a lumber yard in a small town where, in those days, he was equally liked by the few Jews who lived there and the many Gentiles.
I remember as a child, in Hungary the winters were as cruel and cold as probably in the Midwest here.
And we were also selling not only building material, but wood and coal for heating the homes.
And sometimes, some of the women came and-- widows who did not have any money-- and came to ask for some heating material.
And the foreman, the Gentile, said Mr.
Gruenfeld, we are going broke if you keep on giving away the heating material for no money.
But nevertheless, my father believed very strongly that where there is need, that's the overwhelming issue.
And so it was-- however, I remember when the spring came, they really brought their first fruits to our house.
Or if they had a stuffed goose or duck, they brought it as a payment.
So we had nothing but good relationship with the population until about 1936, '37.
But going back a little further, I remember I used to spend my vacation in my grandfather's house.
My grandfather had 11 children.
My mother was the oldest.
And among a few others, I was my grandfather's-- one of the favorite grandchild.
And I spent very pleasant memorable summers with them.

My grandfather, not only was a very learned man in the Talmud, but he was, to that extent, that when there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a court case where the Jews did not want to go to the court of law, but they settled it among themselves, he was called as one of these judges.
And in spite of it, he had the patience to sit down with his grandchildren and play all kinds of games.
And he also had that rare ability that he was teaching us the Talmud in such a manner that we did not realize that we are learning.
He was telling us stories, starting from Adam and Eve.
And it-- later we realized, as we grew older, that they were actually the first introductions to our Talmud studies.
So in spite of it, that he was a very religious man, he was an ardent Zionist, which in those days, among the religious Jews was a no-no.
He was almost criticized among his peers, that how can an orthodox religious Jew be a Zionist?
Because in Palestine in those days, they knew that the Jews were not very religious.
They were not keeping Shabbat.
They were not eating kosher, and so on.
But since 1933, especially when Hitler came to power, he had a foresight that something, something very bad could happen to the Jews.
And again, as an exception, he not only let but even encouraged one of his sons and two of his daughters to go on [NON-ENGLISH] and then to go to Palestine.
And all during the war, we knew that we have some connection to Palestine, even though we had no communication whatsoever with them.
But I'm jumping from--
Since it was a small town, we did-- I did not go to a public school, but six of the children of my age, we had a private teacher who was teaching us all along.

And at the end of the year, we went to the public school and we passed the examination, and this is how we advanced all the way up through the four elementary schools-- grades until we reached gymnasium, which is the junior high, the beginning of the junior high here.
Then I enrolled in the gymnasium and went there for four years.
At the end of the fourth year, which was in '36.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I believe it-- excuse me.
Let me just go back for one second. '25, '31, and eighth year. '39.
Excuse me, 1939 I stopped gonna school because by that time, one of my brothers, who had a nursery, flowers and plants, I took it over because I used to work with him in the summer.
And from that time on, bad times started.
First, one of my brothers, and then two of them, and then all three of them we called into forced labor camp.
I do not know how much time should I--
--Spend on this, but I think I skip it because this is where hard times started.
In 1940, they called my father to the forced labor camp, which at that time, we thought that was a tragedy of immense proportion.
Whoever heard of a man of family should be called to a labor camp?
Nevertheless, he went.
But fortunately, six months later he came back.
And then the war broke out with Russia.
Germany invaded Russia, and they took my-- two of my brothers to the Russian front.
As forced labor camp.
In labor camps.
And we lost contact with them.
I did hear after the war that one of my brother was liberated by the Russians.
But the Russians, instead of separating them from the regular soldiers, they force marched them to-- all the way to Siberia.
During the way, more than half of them died, and then he never came home either.
My younger brother I knew was not far from me in a forced labor camp.
And he was also, I knew, liberated.
And I heard the story after the war that right after the liberation, one of his friends fell into the typhoid fever.
And he was carrying him on his shoulder, so to speak, to a hospital, and took care of him until he recovered.
And then he fell into the same sickness.
But this friend of his did not wait for him or took care of him, and he died there after the war.
Which is again, another irony of fate that, you know, you survive and then you fell ill after the war.
Gyongyos, Hungary.
It is 60 miles southeast of Budapest.
It is not far from the Matra mountains, with is a resort place, was and still is in Hungary.
The home, as I recall, was a very warm, caring, close-knit family home.
And I do believe strongly that one of the reasons I survived was that I remembered and wanted to be back at least one more Friday night with my family.
Friday nights were something, as I recall in the camp always, as a memory where table was covered with white tablecloth, the candles lit, my mother leaning over saying the blessing over the candles.
After that, my father coming home from shul, making kiddish.
And it was an interesting custom there in this small town that if there was a visitor in town, that everybody try to get him for the sabbath.

And whenever it happened that my father brought one home, and unlike here now, that I would have to ask [LAUGHS] my wife before I could bring somebody home, there it was an accepted fact of life, whether my father came home with one or with three.
It was all well and all right.
And then it especially became festive because if it so happened that that person was also [NON-ENGLISH], they used to discuss the Talmud.
And after dinner, this discussion went on and on.
One story brought along another that reminded him on the third.
And us small children just sitting there listening and finally [LAUGHS] almost falling asleep until our mother took us to sleep.
So memories like this really gave us strength to hope and survive.
And we did, some of us.
My mother was not only a very religious woman, but she was immensely well-read.
Not only that, we had quite a sizable library of our own, but she went to the public library and read avidly.
Current, ancient, there was no subject really that one could not talk to her about.
This is not my impression only, but people that I met after the war told me.
And she also had the ability that when we went to sleep, she started to read books, stories, from us, from Anderson, from Grimm, and all those fables.
But later on, she started, when she felt that we're getting sleepy, she turned low the light and started to tell her own stories that she made up.
And those stories actually I remember much longer than the ones that she read from the books.
And she was a very charitable lady.
And last sight I had of her was when they took us-- me out of the ghetto.
I was on one side of the fence, she on the other, and I ran out of the line where we were put in to my mother.
And a Gendarme kicked me on one side, and I saw my mother was dragged away from the other side of the fence.
So this was the last of-- the sight I had of her.
Well, I have to say that from Gyongyos, we moved to a small town, and this is where we had the lumber yard.
This lumber yard we operated until 1941.
When the Jewish laws came out that certain businesses could not be operated by Jews, because they were important for the people's well-being, that time they took it away from us.
And he moved back to Gyongyos.
And there, from 1941 to 1944, we lived there.
And in-- in Gyongyos, there were some 1,200 Jewish souls.
There was an orthodox shul.
Our Rabbi was [NON-ENGLISH] whose niece is [NON-ENGLISH] in New York who started the Hineni movement.
This is a movement where the [NON-ENGLISH] started to get involved with youth who were getting away from Yiddish guide.
And she was very successful to bring many of them back.
So that is about Gyongyos.
Yes.
In the gymnasium, there were some 35 students in a class.
And six of us were Jewish and the rest non-Jewish.
And of course, intimate relationship developed with the Jewish kids much easier and longer-lasting than with the non-Jews, but we did have non-Jews.
I was in the soccer club.
We were playing football with them.
We were in the gymnastics with them.
So yes, there was quite an involved life.
We went swimming with them in the swimming pool and so on.
Yes.
Yes.
In those days, I was playing tennis, going ice skating, swimming.
So until maybe 1942, we were really participating in most of these sports activities also.
Yes.
Well, it was a rather responsible job for 14-year-old boy that was sort of dropped on me.
There were some seven, eight workers whom I had to supervise.
And also, this nursery was operated day and night.
The wintertime, there were ovens that were heating hot water that was circulating in pipes throughout the greenhouse to keep the flowers from freezing.
And that had to be right, because once it frozen, then the flowers would have died.
And in the springtime, we had to take the flowers off to the market and sell them.
So-- and we had some horses and wagon, and take them to the flower shops every so often.
So it was an involved, rather responsible job.
Because by that time, I was left to be the oldest from the children, because all the others were already in labor camps.
However, those labor camps in those days were not as bad as later it became.
They were let out sometimes to come home for a week or ten days.
And we had some correspondence with them.
None of them were married before the war.
So they did not.
We already knew that the war is going on.
We knew that on the Russian front, the situation was very bad, especially because-- at least we thought because of the cold conditions in this.
And even more so, the Jewish forced labor parties because they had no proper clothing and they were exposed to the elements.
And every once in a while came the news that one or another had died and was buried there.
And that was the last we heard of them.
So.
By the Hungarians.
By the Hungarians, yes.
They were digging [SIGHS] ditches for, I guess, tanks.
They were preparing for the Hungarian soldiers lodgings.
They were taking care of horses.
All kinds of manual jobs that the soldiers were saved from doing it.
He was called in '40, but he home after six months.
Yes.
Almost 50.
Yes.
Yes.
This is was-- so difficult to bear.
Not only that he was taken away at that age, but he was physically already-- you know, in those days, a 50-year-old man was an old man.
Yes.
Yes.
In March, 1944, they came into Hungary.
And within a month, with the help of the Hungarian Gendarme and police, rounded up the Jews and forced them into ghettos.
The atmosphere was already very tense.
Our-- my brothers were already in the camps, so we really lived under a lot of strain and stress and worry.
Life was not what it used to be, naturally.
And we did-- In the early '40s and 41, we still had radios, and we listened to the BBC.
And we knew more or less how the war proceeded.
And we were hoping that, first of all, America did not enter the war then yet, [INAUDIBLE] and the moment they entered, we thought that's it, now it will be finished.
Little did we know that it will still drag out for another three-- three and a half-- four years.
So we lived from one day to the other in hope.
Yes.
Yes.
Imre.
My father was not working.
My father actually, once they took away the lumber yard from him, he lost ground because that's what he did all his life.
And he was not very adapt to take up something else.
My mother, on the other hand, was very inventive.
She started to organize a little seamstress school of sort in our home, where we took in work and she was teaching young Jewish girls how to sew.
Because that was a time when intellectual jobs were not available.
No-- Not even office work was allowed for Jewish kids.
So she thought that by training them for a trade, they may be able to make a living.
And in the meantime, she made some money with it too.
So this is how she helped to keep us, us, the family going.
She was a remarkable woman in that sense too, that if one thing did not work out, she was able to switch immediately to something else.
So then that's how you survived from the beginning of the war until 1944.
Yes.
In 1944, from the ghetto, within three weeks, they took us out.
We did not know then that this was actually a lifesaving type of an arrangement.
There was a chief of police in Gyongyos, who, as it turned out after the war, knew what was going on with the Jews in Poland and in Russia.
And he knew already when the ghetto was formed that very shortly they all will be taken to Auschwitz.
First we started to work in some stables, cleaning after the horses in Gyongyos itself, and then they took us, finally the Germans took us to build some the airport for them in [NON-ENGLISH].
No.
For the ghetto, they selected the poorest section of the city where there-- there was a coal mine in Gyongyos and the miners lived in that part of town.
And they used to live some 400 miners there, and they gathered 1,200 Jews in their place.
Yes.
[LAUGHS] Wherever they emptied the Jewish houses.
It was an even exchange.
[LAUGHTER]
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, by the Hungarian Gendarme.
Yes.
There were three families lived in one small house.
One kitchen, one bathroom, and very miserable conditions.
But--
There was at least a roof over our head.
Yes.
Yes, we knew practically every Jewish family in Gyongyos.
Yes.
Well, this was already under terror.
And we did not think, we just functioned, you know, from day to day.
It was instinct that carried already, and the fear of tomorrow always.
Every day, new announcements, new placards, you know, on the walls.
So first--
Jews are not allowed to have more than 200 dollars-- 200 pengos in the Hungarian money, and whoever is found, Jews have to turn over their jewelry.
Jews, they have any radios-- and every day they found something new that they could torment.
It was a planned already well-rehearsed method of terrorizing and intimidating.
We did not realize it then, but we know it now, and this is how they prepared the people that by the time they herded them to the wagons, they really went like sheeps.
But in the meantime, when they took us out and put us into the forced labor camp, we were at that point still, fairly healthy and strong.
They told us if one of you Jews tries to escape or sabotage, you know that we have your families.
So for every Jew who escapes, 100 will be killed.
So they followed by then, to the letter, a well-established form that worked for them.
Poland, Russia, many other countries.
So.
There were some food distributed every morning, some bread.
And besides, we carried in-- I was only four three weeks in the ghetto.
So actually our reserve were still holding us.
And they did give, every morning, something to eat yet in the ghettos.
Not while I was there.

Later I heard, yes, the Zionists, the [NON-ENGLISH] and others started to organize, but they did not have time because all together, by June, from April to June, they liquidated the ghetto completely.
Yes.
Yes.
By edict, and then they came with these, again, on the wall, all kinds of notices and whatnot.
Yes, we did.
We did hide some silver, some gold pieces.
And when we came back, all were gone of course, because they dug up everything.
--now, you were taken out of the ghetto by the police chief, you said.
Yes.
Yes.
Mhm.
